message:
style(search_fts_query_injection): refactor error matching to capture Debug before consuming err

The two FTS injection regression tests added in the previous commit
used a `match err { ... other => panic!("...{other:?}") }` pattern
to report the unexpected-variant case. Logically fine, but structurally
awkward — the `other` arm moves the caught `err` by value and
formats it for the panic message, so if another layer around the
assertion ever needs to inspect `err` after the match, that access
has to be reshuffled.

This commit rewrites both bodies in the same shape:

    let err_dbg = format!("{err:?}");
    let msg = match err {
        frankenterm_core::Error::Storage(StorageError::FtsQueryError(msg)) => msg,
        _ => String::new(),
    };
    assert!(!msg.is_empty(), "unexpected error variant: {err_dbg}");
    assert!(msg.contains(...) || msg.contains(...));

- `err_dbg` is captured eagerly via a Debug format so the diagnostic
  string survives regardless of which match arm fires.
- The match extracts the FTS error's message text when the variant
  is `Storage(FtsQueryError(_))`, otherwise returns an empty
  `String` as a sentinel.
- The first assertion checks the sentinel: if `msg.is_empty()`, the
  variant was wrong and the captured `err_dbg` string is used in
  the panic message, giving exactly the same diagnostic as the old
  `other => panic!` arm. If `msg` is non-empty, we proceed.
- The second assertion runs the substring check against the message
  text, unchanged in intent from the old body.

Both tests (`sqlish_payload_stays_in_structured_fts_error_path` and
`unknown_column_selector_stays_in_structured_fts_error_path`) get
the identical refactor with the expected substring sets preserved
(`"Invalid FTS5 query syntax"`/`"Query validation failed"` for the
sql-injection test; add `"no such column"` for the column-selector
test). No change to what the tests prove; only the control flow
that gets there.
